top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 96, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
GFNY Cozumel's flat coastal island loop with wind exposure rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
When someone attacks on flat coastal island loop with wind exposure, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Race-pace efforts that mimic GFNY Cozumel's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before November.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of GFNY Cozumel. Train it until it feels boring.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 96 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 96 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
The aerobic base that lets you ride flat coastal island loop with wind exposure at tempo for 96 miles without cracking. No shortcuts.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what GFNY Cozumel's flat coastal island loop with wind exposure actually does to you.
GFNY Cozumel's flat coastal island loop with wind exposure forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 96 miles.
Tempo is GFNY Cozumel's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 96 miles on flat coastal island loop with wind exposure. Train it until it's automatic.
At GFNY Cozumel, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for GFNY Cozumel’s 96 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, TT Threshold, and VO2max to prepare you for 96 miles of flat coastal island loop with wind exposure in San Miguel de Cozumel, Quintana Roo, Mexico.
